text: The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and scores the peritoneum near the DJ flexure below the IMV. The plane between the retroperitoneum and mesocolon is exposed. The peritoneum below the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is incised to access the posterior plane of TMA. The IMA is traced cranially to its root, where nodal dissection is performed for complete clearance of nodal tissue. The IMA is transected beyond the origin of the left colic artery, followed by medial to lateral mobilization of the mesocolon and separation from the retroperitoneum. The posterior plane of DME is dissected sharply and bluntly, extending to the pelvic floor, with further dissection planned from the perineal side.

The surgeon focuses on the left lateral side, mobilizing the ureter and identifying its passage below the vas deferens. The vas deferens is mobilized, and the artery supplying it is identified. Both the ureter and the artery to the vas deferens are clipped and divided.

The surgeon communicates the lateral and posterior compartments of the mesorectum, creating an enterolateral plane. The seminal vesicles are separated from the posterior bladder wall, and dissection proceeds anteriorly. The lateral ligament of the rectum is divided using bipolar forceps and scissors to prevent bleeding. On the right side, the vas deferens and its artery are identified, clipped, and divided. The plane between the bladder and seminal vesicle is created, with dissection carried out anterior to the seminal vesicle and posterior to the bladder, taking care to avoid entering the bladder.

The surgeon matches the enterolateral planes anteriorly, proceeding to the base of the bladder neck. On the right side, the lateral ligament of the rectum is dissected, completing the right lateral dissection until reaching the pelvic floor.

A gauze piece is placed anteriorly, and the sigmoid colon is divided to create an end colostomy. The surgeon identifies and enters the space of Retzius, then sharply divides the bilateral puboprostatic ligaments after outlining the prostate contour. The bladder neck is divided at the prostate apex until the urethral catheter is visible. Assistance is provided to continuously suction urine and blood. The urethra is retracted and held, while the posterior wall of the bladder neck is divided, revealing the previously placed gauze piece at the seminal vesicle level.

The prostate is suspended by the neurovascular bundles, which are identified and controlled using bipolar cautery. The surgeon closes the bladder neck with a V-lock suture in two layers. A permanent suprapubic catheter (SPC) is inserted through a new incision into the bladder, secured with a suture, and anchored to the anterior abdominal wall. The remaining dissection is completed from the perineal side.
